adults.explanation: Revelation 19:15,21 — "Out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations... the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth." Christ's word alone — creative, judicial, irresistible — is His only weapon.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the beast and false prophet being the first occupants of the lake of fire?
adults.option_a: They precede even Satan (who joins them in 20:10) — the anti-trinity's two earthly agents are judged before their master; their being cast "alive" emphasises conscious, ongoing judgment; the lake of fire becomes the final destiny of everything opposed to God
adults.option_d: Only Satan enters the lake of fire
adults.explanation: Revelation 19:20 — "Cast alive into a lake of fire." The beast and false prophet are the first to enter (still there in 20:10 — "where the beast and the false prophet are"), establishing the lake of fire as a place of conscious, eternal punishment for God's enemies.

adults.question: What does the angel's rebuke "worship God" when John falls at his feet reveal?
adults.option_a: Even in the overwhelming context of heavenly glory, worship belongs to God alone — the angel identifies himself as a "fellowservant" of John and the prophets; this anti-worship reflex (repeated in 22:8-9) reinforces monotheistic worship as Revelation's central ethical demand
adults.option_c: John was wrong to worship at all
adults.explanation: Revelation 19:10 — "See thou do it not: I am thy fellowservant... worship God." This correction (repeated in 22:8-9) underscores Revelation's central message: worship God alone. Even the most glorious heavenly being refuses worship — only God and the Lamb receive it.

adults.question: How does "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy" define the relationship between testimony and prophecy?
adults.option_a: All true prophecy centres on and testifies to Jesus — the Holy Spirit's prophetic activity is fundamentally christocentric; every genuine prophetic word, whether in Scripture or the church, has Jesus as its essential content and goal
adults.option_b: Prophecy has nothing to do with Jesus
adults.explanation: Revelation 19:10 — "The testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy." This programmatic statement means that the animating purpose (spirit/pneuma) of all prophecy is testimony about Jesus. Christ is not just one prophetic topic among many — He is the sum and substance of all prophetic revelation.

kids.question: What happened to Satan for a thousand years?
kids.option_a: An angel bound him with a great chain and locked him in the bottomless pit for a thousand years
kids.explanation: Revelation 20:1-3 — "And he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the Devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years, And cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up."

kids.question: What happens at the great white throne judgment?
kids.option_a: Everyone is judged according to their works, and the books are opened including the book of life
kids.explanation: Revelation 20:12 — "And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works."

kids.question: What is the last enemy that is thrown into the lake of fire?
kids.option_a: Death and hell (hades) — they are completely destroyed
kids.explanation: Revelation 20:14 — "And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death."

teens.question: What happened after Satan was released from the thousand years?
teens.option_a: He went out to deceive the nations (Gog and Magog) to gather them for battle, but fire from heaven devoured them
teens.explanation: Revelation 20:7-9 — "And when the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison, And shall go out to deceive the nations... Gog, and Magog, to gather them together to battle... And fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them."

teens.question: Where was Satan finally thrown?
teens.option_a: Into the lake of fire and brimstone where the beast and false prophet are, to be tormented day and night for ever and ever
teens.explanation: Revelation 20:10 — "And the devil that deceived them was cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are, and shall be tormented day and night for ever and ever."

teens.question: Who sat on the thrones during the thousand years?
teens.option_a: Those who had been beheaded for Jesus' testimony and God's word, who had not worshipped the beast — they lived and reigned with Christ
teens.explanation: Revelation 20:4 — "And I saw thrones, and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, and which had not worshipped the beast... and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years."

teens.question: What is the second death?
teens.option_a: The lake of fire — anyone whose name was not found in the book of life was cast into it
teens.explanation: Revelation 20:14-15 — "And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death. And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire."

teens.question: What is the "first resurrection"?
teens.option_a: The resurrection of those who reign with Christ during the thousand years — "Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power"
teens.option_b: The resurrection of all people at once
teens.explanation: Revelation 20:5-6 — "This is the first resurrection. Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years."

adults.question: What are the major interpretive positions on the millennium?
adults.option_a: Premillennialism (Christ returns before a literal 1,000-year reign), amillennialism (the 1,000 years symbolise the present church age with Christ reigning in heaven), and postmillennialism (Christ returns after the church progressively Christianises the world) — each has substantial exegetical support
adults.option_b: There is only one valid interpretation
adults.option_c: The millennium is not mentioned in Revelation
adults.option_d: All scholars agree on the meaning
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:1-6 — The three main positions reflect different hermeneutical approaches to apocalyptic numbers and the sequence of events. Premillennialism reads the text sequentially; amillennialism sees recapitulation; postmillennialism emphasises kingdom growth.

adults.question: How does "Gog and Magog" connect to Ezekiel 38-39?
adults.option_a: Ezekiel 38-39 describes a future invasion of restored Israel by Gog from the land of Magog — Revelation universalises this: Gog and Magog become symbols for all nations gathered against God's people in the final rebellion, transcending the original geographical reference
adults.option_b: There is no connection to Ezekiel
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:8 — "Gog and Magog, to gather them together to battle: the number of whom is as the sand of the sea." John takes Ezekiel's specific northern enemy and universalises it — Gog and Magog become a comprehensive symbol for anti-God humanity.

adults.question: What does the great white throne judgment reveal about final accountability?
adults.option_a: Universal, comprehensive judgment — "the dead, small and great" stand before God; the "books" record works (basis of judgment), while the "book of life" determines destiny; the judgment is both according to works (evaluative) and according to the book of life (salvific)
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:12-13 — "The dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works." Two sets of records: books of works (what people did) and the book of life (whose names are inscribed). Both are consulted — works demonstrate faith's reality; the book of life secures destiny.

adults.question: What does the casting of death and Hades into the lake of fire signify?
adults.option_a: The permanent abolition of death itself — the last enemy (1 Corinthians 15:26) is destroyed; death and its realm (Hades) are personified and judged, meaning mortality and the intermediate state are terminated; from this point, only eternal life and the second death exist
adults.option_b: Death continues in a different form
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:14 — "Death and hell were cast into the lake of fire." 1 Corinthians 15:26 — "The last enemy that shall be destroyed is death." Death itself dies — the ultimate consequence of sin (Romans 6:23) is itself consumed, making the new creation truly deathless (21:4).

adults.question: How does "the second death hath no power" over first-resurrection participants function?
adults.option_a: Those who share in the first resurrection (whether physical or spiritual, depending on millennial view) are permanently immune from the lake of fire — they are "priests of God and of Christ," combining Exodus 19:6's royal-priestly vocation with eternal security
adults.option_b: The second death has power over everyone
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:6 — "Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power." This is the fifth of Revelation's seven beatitudes, guaranteeing eternal security for those who belong to Christ.

adults.question: What does Satan's release and final rebellion demonstrate?
adults.option_a: That a thousand years of bondage does not reform evil — Satan immediately resumes deception; and that human nature, even after prolonged peace, is susceptible to rebellion apart from grace; the final revolt demonstrates the inexhaustibility of evil and the necessity of its permanent removal
adults.option_d: Evil no longer exists after the binding
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:7-8 — Satan's immediate resumption of deception after a thousand years proves that restraint does not equal transformation. The brief final rebellion serves to demonstrate conclusively the incorrigible nature of evil before its eternal sentence.

adults.question: How does fire from heaven consuming the final rebellion echo Ezekiel 38-39?
adults.option_a: Ezekiel 39:6 — "I will send a fire on Magog" — God's direct intervention by fire from heaven destroys the armies surrounding "the camp of the saints and the beloved city" without any human military action; divine judgment is immediate, total, and sovereign
adults.option_b: There is no fire in Ezekiel
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:9 — "Fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them." cf. Ezekiel 38:22; 39:6 — fire and brimstone on Gog. The pattern of divine intervention by fire (cf. Genesis 19:24 — Sodom; 2 Kings 1:10 — Elijah) shows God alone executes the final victory.

adults.question: What does "tormented day and night for ever and ever" establish about the nature of final judgment?
adults.option_a: It describes conscious, eternal punishment — the language "day and night" (continuous) and "for ever and ever" (eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — unto the ages of the ages) is the strongest possible expression of unending duration in Greek, the same phrase used for God's own eternal existence (4:9-10)
adults.explanation: Revelation 20:10 — "Tormented day and night for ever and ever." The phrase eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn is used of God's eternal life (4:9-10; 10:6; 15:7), the Lamb's worship (5:13), and now the punishment of the devil, beast, and false prophet — same duration in every case.

kids.question: What did John see coming down from heaven?
kids.option_a: A new Jerusalem — a beautiful holy city prepared as a bride adorned for her husband
kids.explanation: Revelation 21:2 — "And I John saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband."

kids.question: What will God wipe away?
kids.option_a: All tears from their eyes — there will be no more death, sorrow, crying, or pain
kids.explanation: Revelation 21:4 — "And God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes; and there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain: for the former things are passed away."

kids.question: What were the twelve gates of the new city made of?
kids.option_a: Each gate was a single pearl — the gates of pearl
kids.explanation: Revelation 21:21 — "And the twelve gates were twelve pearls: every several gate was of one pearl."

teens.question: What declaration does God make from His throne?
teens.option_a: "Behold, I make all things new" — everything is being made fresh and perfect
teens.explanation: Revelation 21:5 — "And he that sat upon the throne said, Behold, I make all things new."

teens.question: What is notably absent from the new Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: There is no temple — because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple
teens.explanation: Revelation 21:22 — "And I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it."

teens.question: What provides light in the new Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: The glory of God and the Lamb — there is no need for the sun or moon
teens.explanation: Revelation 21:23 — "And the city had no need of the sun, neither of the moon, to shine in it: for the glory of God did lighten it, and the Lamb is the light thereof."

teens.question: What are the foundations of the city walls decorated with?
teens.option_a: Twelve precious stones — jasper, sapphire, chalcedony, emerald, sardonyx, sardius, chrysolyte, beryl, topaz, chrysoprasus, jacinth, and amethyst
teens.explanation: Revelation 21:19-20 — "And the foundations of the wall of the city were garnished with all manner of precious stones."

teens.question: Who will NOT enter the new Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Nothing that defiles, works abomination, or makes a lie — only those written in the Lamb's book of life
teens.explanation: Revelation 21:27 — "And there shall in no wise enter into it any thing that defileth, neither whatsoever worketh abomination, or maketh a lie: but they which are written in the Lamb's book of life."

adults.question: How does "a new heaven and a new earth" fulfil Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22?
adults.option_a: Isaiah prophesied God creating "new heavens and a new earth" — Revelation shows the fulfilment: not merely renovation but transformation; "the first heaven and the first earth were passed away" indicates the radical newness of the eschatological order while maintaining continuity with creation's original purpose
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:1 — "I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away." Isaiah 65:17 — "For, behold, I create new heavens and a new earth." The Greek kainos means qualitatively new (not neos — chronologically new) — renewed and perfected rather than replaced.

adults.question: What does "the sea was no more" signify?
adults.option_a: The sea in Revelation symbolises chaos, the source of the beast (13:1), and separation from God — its abolition signifies the end of all chaotic, threatening, and separating forces; the primordial chaos is finally and permanently subdued
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:1 — "And there was no more sea." In the ancient Near Eastern worldview and in Revelation specifically, the sea represents chaos (Isaiah 57:20), the abyss (Revelation 11:7; 13:1), and danger. Its removal means all forces of chaos, evil, and separation are permanently eliminated.

adults.question: How does "the tabernacle of God is with men" fulfil the entire biblical narrative?
adults.option_a: It consummates the trajectory from Eden (God walking with Adam), through the tabernacle (Exodus 25:8 — "that I may dwell among them"), the temple, the incarnation (John 1:14 — "tabernacled among us"), to the eternal dwelling — the goal of all redemptive history is achieved: unmediated divine-human communion
adults.option_c: God has always dwelt with humanity
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:3 — "Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them, and they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God." This is the Bible's final answer to its opening question: God and humanity together.

adults.question: What does the city's cubic shape (equal length, breadth, and height) signify?
adults.option_a: The Holy of Holies in Solomon's temple was a perfect cube (1 Kings 6:20 — 20 cubits each dimension) — the entire new Jerusalem is a Holy of Holies, meaning every part of the city is the most sacred space; all God's people live in His immediate, unmediated presence
adults.option_d: Cubes were common in ancient cities
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:16 — "The length and the breadth and the height of it are equal." The only perfect cube in Scripture is the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:20). The entire city IS the Holy of Holies — the entire new creation is God's immediate dwelling place.

adults.question: How do the twelve gates and twelve foundations connect Israel and the church?
adults.option_a: The twelve gates bear the names of the twelve tribes of Israel, and the twelve foundations bear the names of the twelve apostles — the city unites God's people from both testaments into one eschatological community; there are not two separate peoples but one redeemed humanity
adults.option_b: Israel and the church are separate cities
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:12-14 — Gates = twelve tribes of Israel (entry); foundations = twelve apostles (basis). The city's structure integrates Old and New Testament people of God into one unified community — the patriarchs are the entry points, the apostles are the foundation.

adults.question: What does the absence of a temple signify?
adults.option_a: No mediating structure is needed because "the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple" — the entire city is a temple (cube-shaped Holy of Holies); the temple's function (enabling divine-human encounter) is universalised; the symbol is transcended by the reality
adults.option_d: A temple will be built later
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:22 — "I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it." Throughout Scripture, temples facilitated meeting God. Now God's presence is so pervasive that no sacred-secular distinction exists — everywhere is the Holy of Holies.

adults.question: How does "the nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it" envision the new creation?
adults.option_a: It fulfils Isaiah 60:3,11 (nations walking in Zion's light, gates never shut) — the new Jerusalem draws the nations into God's glory; diversity ("nations") is preserved in eternity; ethnic and cultural richness is not abolished but redeemed and brought into God's light
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:24,26 — "The nations of them which are saved shall walk in the light of it: and the kings of the earth do bring their glory and honour into it... And they shall bring the glory and honour of the nations into it." Cultural diversity is eternally preserved and consecrated.

adults.question: What does "Behold, I make all things new" reveal about the nature of the new creation?
adults.option_a: God does not make "all new things" but makes "all things new" — transformation, not replacement; continuity with the original creation is maintained but everything is renewed and perfected; the material world is redeemed, not discarded, fulfilling Romans 8:19-22's hope of creation's liberation
adults.explanation: Revelation 21:5 — "Behold, I make all things new (kaina)." The word order is significant: not "new things" but "all things new." The creational material is preserved and transformed — resurrection theology (continuity + transformation) applied to the entire cosmos.

kids.question: What flows through the middle of the city?
kids.option_a: A pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, flowing from God's throne
kids.explanation: Revelation 22:1 — "And he shewed me a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb."

kids.question: What grows on either side of the river?
kids.option_a: The tree of life, which bears twelve kinds of fruit and whose leaves are for the healing of the nations
kids.explanation: Revelation 22:2 — "In the midst of the street of it, and on either side of the river, was there the tree of life, which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month: and the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations."

kids.question: What does Jesus say at the very end of the book?
kids.option_a: "Surely I come quickly" — and John replies "Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus"
kids.option_c: "I will return in a thousand years"
kids.explanation: Revelation 22:20 — "He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly. Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus."

teens.question: What is no longer in the new Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: No more curse — the curse from Eden is completely removed
teens.explanation: Revelation 22:3 — "And there shall be no more curse: but the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it; and his servants shall serve him."

teens.question: What will God's servants see?
teens.option_a: His face — they shall see His face, and His name shall be in their foreheads
teens.explanation: Revelation 22:4 — "And they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads."

teens.question: What invitation is given at the end of the book?
teens.option_a: "The Spirit and the bride say, Come. And let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come. And whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely"
teens.explanation: Revelation 22:17 — "And the Spirit and the bride say, Come. And let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come. And whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely."

teens.question: What warning is given about adding to or taking away from this book?
teens.option_a: If anyone adds to these words, God will add plagues to him; if anyone takes away, God will take away his part from the book of life and the holy city
teens.option_d: The warning applies to other books
teens.explanation: Revelation 22:18-19 — "If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book: And if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life."

teens.question: What does Jesus call Himself in the last chapter?
teens.option_a: "I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star"
teens.option_c: "I am the king of Israel only"
teens.explanation: Revelation 22:16 — "I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches. I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star."

adults.question: How does the river of life and tree of life reverse the Genesis curse?
adults.option_a: In Eden, the tree of life was guarded by cherubim after the fall (Genesis 3:22-24); now access is fully restored — the river of life (cf. Ezekiel 47:1-12; Genesis 2:10) flows freely, the tree bears continuous fruit, and its leaves heal the nations; every consequence of the fall is undone
adults.option_c: The tree is different from Eden's
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:1-3 — The river echoes Eden's river (Genesis 2:10) and Ezekiel's temple river (47:1-12). The tree of life (Genesis 2:9; 3:22-24) is now accessible. "No more curse" (v.3) explicitly reverses Genesis 3:17. The Bible ends where it began — paradise, but perfected.

adults.question: What does "they shall see his face" (the beatific vision) represent theologically?
adults.option_a: The ultimate fulfilment of human destiny — Moses was denied God's face (Exodus 33:20); the psalmist longed for it (Psalm 17:15; 27:4); Jesus promised it (Matthew 5:8); now it is realised; the beatific vision is unmediated communion with God — the supreme blessing of the new creation
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:4 — "They shall see his face." Exodus 33:20 — "There shall no man see me, and live." Psalm 17:15 — "I shall behold thy face in righteousness." Matthew 5:8 — "The pure in heart... shall see God." What was forbidden becomes the eternal privilege of the redeemed.

adults.question: How does "the root and the offspring of David" compress Christ's identity?
adults.option_a: He is simultaneously David's ancestor (root — the source from which David sprang, alluding to Isaiah 11:1,10 and His pre-existence) and David's descendant (offspring — born in David's line, fulfilling 2 Samuel 7:12-13); He is both before David and after David — eternal God and incarnate man
adults.option_d: The title has no christological significance
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:16 — "I am the root and the offspring of David." Root = source/origin (pre-existence, deity); offspring = descendant (incarnation, humanity). The dual title echoes Jesus' question in Matthew 22:42-45 — how can David's son also be David's Lord?

adults.question: How does "the Spirit and the bride say, Come" function as the book's final invitation?
adults.option_a: It is a multilayered invitation: (1) the Spirit and bride invite Christ to return ("Come"), (2) hearers pass the invitation along, (3) the thirsty are invited to come to Christ for the water of life; it combines Maranatha eschatology with evangelistic offer — longing for Christ's return and welcoming the lost simultaneously
adults.option_b: It is addressed only to angels
adults.option_c: It invites people to a literal feast
adults.option_d: It is a command to leave
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:17 — The threefold "Come" addresses both Christ (eschatological longing) and the unconverted (evangelistic invitation). The Spirit's internal call and the bride's external testimony unite in offering the water of life "freely" (dōrean — as a gift, cf. Romans 3:24).

adults.question: What does the warning against adding to or taking from "this prophecy" protect?
adults.option_a: It protects the integrity of the prophetic revelation — echoing Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32 (do not add to or diminish God's commands); it treats Revelation as authoritative Scripture on par with Torah and demands that its message be preserved intact
adults.option_b: It applies to all books ever written
adults.option_c: It is a later scribal addition
adults.option_d: It protects only the last chapter
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:18-19 — "If any man shall add... if any man shall take away." Cf. Deuteronomy 4:2; 12:32; Proverbs 30:6. The imprecatory warnings (plagues added, name removed) make altering the text an act of sacrilege — the strongest possible canonical protection.

adults.question: How does "Surely I come quickly" (Erchomai tachy) summarise Revelation's eschatology?
adults.option_a: It is Christ's third and final promise of imminent return (cf. 22:7,12,20); "quickly" (tachy) emphasises suddenness and certainty rather than chronological immediacy — when Christ comes, He comes swiftly and decisively; the church lives in perpetual readiness
adults.option_b: It means within one calendar year
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:20 — "Surely I come quickly." The adverb tachy means "soon/suddenly/swiftly" — emphasising the certainty and sudden nature of the return rather than specifying a timeline. The church's response — "Even so, come, Lord Jesus" — is the final prayer of Scripture.

adults.question: How does Revelation 22 bring the entire biblical canon to its intended conclusion?
adults.option_a: It reverses every curse of Genesis 3 (no more curse), restores Eden's features (river, tree of life), fulfils Israel's hopes (new Jerusalem, nations healed), completes Christ's work (throne of God and the Lamb), and answers the Bible's first question of broken fellowship with the ultimate answer: "They shall see his face"
adults.option_c: The Bible has no unified narrative
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:1-5 reverses Genesis 3 point by point: river (Gen 2:10), tree of life (Gen 2:9; 3:24), no curse (Gen 3:17), God's presence (Gen 3:8), seeing God's face (Gen 3:23-24). The Bible's narrative arc from creation to fall to redemption to restoration is complete.

adults.question: What does "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all" reveal about Revelation's genre and purpose?
adults.option_a: The epistolary closing (like Paul's letters) reminds readers that Revelation is not merely apocalyptic spectacle but a pastoral letter to churches — it begins (1:4) and ends with grace; despite the terrifying judgments, the final word is grace, framing the entire book within the gospel
adults.explanation: Revelation 22:21 — "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen." Revelation opens as a letter (1:4 — "Grace be unto you, and peace") and closes as one. The entire apocalypse is bookended by grace — the first and last word to the churches is the gospel of grace.
